## 来源说明

以下证据来自 Apple Podcasts US Top Shows 官方榜单、iTunes lookup 得到的 RSS feed、节目 RSS 元数据及其可用 transcript。候选按 Apple 榜单顺序保留；每个节目只选择近 10 天内第一个未归档且可转写的 episode。没有 RSS、近期音频或 transcript 的条目已在 source 阶段跳过。AI 只能依据 transcript 与元数据写作；不得编造嘉宾、观点或未提供的事实。

## 候选播客清单

### 1. Why local news keeps disappearing

- Apple Top Shows 排名：#1
- Apple ID：1200361736
- Apple 页面：https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/the-daily/id1200361736
- Apple 分类：News / Daily News
- 节目：The Daily
- 来源：The New York Times
- 嘉宾：未标明
- 发布日期：2099-01-02
- 链接：https://example.com/the-daily-local-news
- 音频：https://example.com/audio/the-daily.mp3
- Show notes：A conversation about local news economics, advertising collapse, ownership changes and civic information gaps.

#### Transcript

The host explains that the old local newspaper model depended on a bundle: classified ads, local retail ads, subscriptions and a newsroom that covered city hall, courts and schools. Digital platforms broke that bundle. Classifieds moved to search and marketplace sites, local stores shifted budget to targeted ads, and readers stopped treating the daily paper as the default place for civic information. The guest says the result is not only fewer reporters; it is a weaker public memory of what happened in a town last year, who promised what, and which decisions produced visible outcomes.

The episode uses a county budget fight as an example. When no reporter attended meetings for months, officials described a tax change as a technical adjustment. Residents only noticed after bills arrived. The guest argues that journalism works like maintenance infrastructure: when it is present, it looks ordinary; when it disappears, the failure shows up later as rumor, lower trust and slower accountability. The host asks whether newsletters and independent creators can replace newspapers. The answer is mixed: they can cover passionate niches, but the work of routine public records, court filings and school boards is repetitive and hard to fund through enthusiasm alone.

### 2. How prediction markets turned uncertainty into a product

- Apple Top Shows 排名：#2
- Apple ID：290783428
- Apple 页面：https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/planet-money/id290783428
- Apple 分类：Business / News
- 节目：Planet Money
- 来源：NPR
- 嘉宾：未标明
- 发布日期：2099-01-02
- 链接：https://example.com/planet-money-prediction-markets
- 音频：https://example.com/audio/planet-money.mp3
- Show notes：The episode explains how prediction markets price uncertain events and why regulation treats them differently from ordinary polling.

#### Transcript

The reporter opens with the difference between asking people what they think and asking them to put money behind a forecast. A poll measures stated preference or belief at a moment. A prediction market creates a price that moves when traders believe new information changes the probability of an event. The guest says the useful part is not that markets are magical truth machines. The useful part is that a price forces disagreement into a comparable number, and that number can update quickly when incentives are designed well.

The episode also stresses the boundary. Thin markets can be distorted by a few large traders, and events with ambiguous settlement rules can create fights over what actually happened. Regulation becomes difficult because the same contract can look like information aggregation, gambling, financial speculation or political influence depending on context. The reporter uses weather, elections and product launch dates to show that prediction markets are most informative when the event is clearly defined, the participant pool is broad, and the payout rule is not easy to manipulate.

### 3. Why sleep advice fails when it ignores work schedules

- Apple Top Shows 排名：#3
- Apple ID：1545953110
- Apple 页面：https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/health-show-example/id1545953110
- Apple 分类：Health & Fitness / Education
- 节目：Health Lab
- 来源：Example Health Media
- 嘉宾：未标明
- 发布日期：2099-01-02
- 链接：https://example.com/health-lab-sleep-work
- 音频：https://example.com/audio/health-lab.mp3
- Show notes：A discussion about sleep routines, shift work, family constraints and why generic wellness advice often fails.

#### Transcript

The host begins by asking why people know the basic sleep advice but still fail to follow it. The guest says the advice is often written for someone who controls their evening, their bedroom and their work start time. Many workers do not. Shift schedules, caregiving, long commutes and noisy housing change sleep from a personal discipline problem into a coordination problem. The guest does not reject routines; they argue that routines only work when the surrounding constraints are visible.

The episode separates two ideas that are often confused. Sleep hygiene means reducing friction around sleep: light, caffeine timing, screens, temperature and consistent cues. Sleep opportunity means having enough protected time in which sleep can happen. A person can improve hygiene and still fail if opportunity is too short or fragmented. The host uses a nurse schedule to show the difference: black-out curtains and caffeine rules help, but rotating shifts still move the body clock faster than it can adapt. The practical lesson is to diagnose the constraint before prescribing the habit.

## 写作边界

- 这是基于 Apple Top Shows 热门节目近期 episode transcript 的中文长文笔记，不是榜单介绍或节目推荐。
- 需要按输入顺序逐条展开，不要额外生成总览或播客清单；不要把多个节目合并成一个主题。
- 若 transcript 或元数据没有明确嘉宾姓名，嘉宾字段写“未标明”，不要猜。
- 每条分析必须能回到 transcript 证据；不得仅凭榜单排名、标题、链接、图片或简短简介扩写。
- 不要生成金融建议、健康建议、产品购买建议或夸张标题。
